title: Dragon Strike (1993) - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UhuJQBAZv84

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens his vintage-games series with Dragon Strike, TSR's 1993 attempt to answer the HeroQuest phenomenon and squeeze basic D&D onto toy-store shelves — the one infamous for its gloriously terrible pack-in VHS. He argues the video, prominently featured on the box, may have hurt it, since this is genuinely a board-game-meets-intro-to-roleplaying: players are nudged to question monsters, puzzle out secrets, disarm traps and attempt feats, with the adventure book feeding the dungeon master example answers.

He rates it a more complex, more rewarding game than HeroQuest — using polyhedral dice and to-hit rolls — even if HeroQuest is the more cohesive package with stronger art. The box is generous: a well-written rulebook that teaches roleplaying to total newcomers, an adventure book of 16 quests, a DM map book, a useful screen, decks of spells, treasure, traps and monster stats, and four double-sided boards (caverns, castle, city, valley) that chain into a mini-campaign. His gripes are cosmetic: the VHS-still character art is dreadful and the minis are stiff, two-dimensional poses (his dragon's wing snapped off). Verdict: a genuinely interesting design he'd happily run today as a simple one-off D&D night.

----

Daniel makes the case that Dragon Strike is a better *game* than HeroQuest, even if HeroQuest wins on art and cohesion. Did you grow up with either — and which one earns the nostalgia in your memory?
